test_cases.input: Article excerpt from an investigation into how long-haul trucking companies underpay drivers through lease-to-own equipment deals. Strong quotes available. Platforms: X/Twitter and LinkedIn. Publication handle provided. No paywall.
test_cases.output_summary: Selected the strongest quote from a driver: 'After two years, I owed the company more than when I started.' X post worked as a standalone 240-character tweet. LinkedIn version correctly added labor/business context before the quote. Attribution clean. No paraphrase added to the excerpt — preserved journalism voice. Posts did not oversell beyond the article's claims.

test_cases.input: Article: Long-form essay on the decline of the family dinner as a social institution. No data, no quotes — primarily argumentative/essayistic. Platforms: Instagram and LinkedIn.
test_cases.output_summary: Correctly identified that without quotes or statistics, the most shareable element was the essay's central thesis statement. Instagram quote card was a clean distillation of the argument. LinkedIn framing added sociological context. One quote card selection was slightly long for the 200-character guideline (218 characters) — minor flag. Caption on Instagram appropriately conversational. Did not fabricate a quote or statistic.

test_cases.input: Article: Short news report (300 words) on a city council vote approving a new transit line. Minimal narrative, mostly factual. Platforms: X/Twitter only. No paywall.
test_cases.output_summary: Correctly identified this as thin source material for social excerpting. Generated a clean X post from the lead statistic. Flagged in usage note that the article lacks strong narrative hooks for social and suggested the journalist add a reaction quote if one becomes available. Did not fabricate content. Post was accurate to the article. Practical limitation honestly noted.
